title: Heap Sequence That Outlives Its Maker
prompt_md:
## Goal

A stack array dies the instant the function that created it returns, so it can never be handed back to the caller. The heap has no such restriction. Use that fact here.

Write a function that allocates an array of `n` integers **on the heap**, fills slot `i` with the value `(i + 1)^2`, and returns a pointer to it. `main` reads `n`, calls your function, prints the values, and must `delete[]` the array before exiting (no leaks). The printing is already wired up -- your work is the heap allocation and the matching cleanup.

## Input

- A single integer `n` (1 <= n <= 100).

## Output

- One line: the `n` squares, space-separated.

title: Heap Copy You Must Free
prompt_md:
## Goal

The whole point of this exercise is the **heap lifecycle**: allocate with `new[]`, hand the buffer back to a caller that outlives the allocating function, use it, then `delete[]` it.

Write a function `copyToHeap(const int* values, int n)` that allocates an array of `n` ints **on the heap**, copies the `n` source values into it, and returns the pointer -- so the copy outlives the call. `main` reads the values into a local (stack) array, hands them to your function, prints the heap copy, then frees it. The reading and printing are scaffolded; your work is the allocation, the copy, and the matching `delete[]`.

## Input

- Line 1: a positive integer `n` (1 <= n <= 100).
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers.

## Output

- One line: the `n` values (from the heap copy), space-separated.
